sentences.text: No.
Well, I-- no, not in per se, as a many, you know, as a group.
But I put on my tallit every morning.
I I was hiding it.
As a matter of fact, I have a little siddur that I can't find-- I was looking for it, high and low-- that I have dates put in that I carried through concentration camp with me.
Most people didn't.
In secret, yes.
I don't know.
I really-- nothing, really nothing much.
They may have taken it away from me.
But I was amongst in a barrack, early in the morning.
And nobody-- nobody would squeal on me.
That I put on a tallit.
No.
No.
There was this doctor, or lawyer, whatever he was.
He tried to-- you know, he spoke German.
And he tried to be our leader.
But he didn't do a good job at it.
No, well--
He wanted to be himself.
He wanted to be himself, a leader.
And this way, he didn't have to work like the others worked.
And the work was pretty hard, especially for the women.
Where they put us out to a field to pick sugar beets.
And sugar beets are quite big, and they have some big leaves.
And each person was given three rows to pick, knock it together, and throw it in a bunch.
And if you couldn't keep up, the Ukrainian guy was there with a whip behind you.
And in order for nobody to touch my mother and my two sisters, I picked theirs, helped them, so they can-- they don't fall behind.
And I picked my own, and I picked another man's because he gave me an extra piece of bread.
How I did it, I don't know.
But God gave me strength, I guess.
Because if he would ever touch my mother, I probably wouldn't be here today.
Yes.
[SIGHS] I drove a wagon into Vienna.
I drove a horse and wagon into Vienna.
I was very good with animals, with horses.
To deliver the potatoes to military camps, to the Germans.
While the bombs were falling, I was on the street of Vienna.
I used to clean the stables.
They looked for a volunteer to shovel out the latrine for extra food.
One other person.
It was from the-- from the farm.
No, no.
No guard, no guard.
Yeah, yeah, no guard.
They trusted me, because I left a deposit, my mother and my two sisters.
And if I didn't come back, I was told, you know, if you don't come back--
That something will happen--
--that's the end of them.
So you know well that I'm going to come back.
Yeah, well, I cleaned stables.
I milk cows.
At times, I smelled so bad I had to sleep in the stable.
I couldn't go in a barrack, cleaning the latrines, for the extra food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Work.
I used to use a pitchfork, you know, to wheelbarrow out the manure, to-- out to make a mountain out of it.
I shoveled coal, filling and loading freight cars, with coal, the big-- a big coal shovel.
Whatever there was-- when work had to be done, I've done it to stay alive.
[DRINKS]
[SIGHS] That's basically about it.
I would-- I would say the summer of '44 till the early-- till the fall, late fall.
Because when it got cold, they took us to the front lines.
I know-- I know the cold weather came then.
Because in-- in the farm when we were there, it wasn't cold.
We didn't need no heat.
After that, they put us in a train, [SIGHS] all 80 of us, and took us to a-- a small town at the Hungarian-Austrian border, that was a still a part of Austria.
And we were digging tank traps.
So it took us-- every morning we had to walk up-- there-- there, things got nasty.
I can't.
Well it was '44 into '45, or I guess '44.
It's too far to remember back, you know, what what the dates were.
Because who kept dates?
Who knew what the date was?
We didn't even know what day it was at times.
This was not too far from Hungary, from the border of Hungary.
No.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was a small place.
But it was up in the hills in the mountains, almost like the Alps type of thing, bitter cold.
We had to walk up every morning, up to the mountains, with picks, and shovels and saws to clear areas.
So when the tanks come up, they would fall into it.
And while we were working up there, we could see the planes come and bomb the railroad station down below, where the Hungarians were running away from the Russians.
And when the train pulled into the station, we were so close to the-- to the planes, up on top of the mountain, that we could see the face of the pilot.
And we stood out there with our yellow star, so they could see who we are.
And they used to dive down into the railroad station with machine guns and pick the people off as they were jumping out the windows at the railroad station.
And there I fell off a mountains, I broke my left wrist.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I couldn't work.
When that happened I was carrying wood for firewood.
And it was icy, snowy, and I slipped and just rolled off, and when I-- when I finally landed my left hand was wound up in back of me with my wrist broken.
And they was going to take me up to the hills, and do away with me.
But my mother begged them to send me to a hospital to put a cast on.
Because there was somebody else there with a broken arm.
So finally they-- a nurse came.
They took me to a hospital, where they X-rayed it.
And this German doctor picked on me that I had an American flag around my neck.
I had a scarf.
I didn't even know what American flag looks like.
No.
I didn't even know what a flag looks like, American flag.
It was a scarf that my mother gave me, that I should-- I should be warm.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- this German doctor just tried to do away with me.
He-- he tortured me in a sense to humiliate me.
He took me into the operating room, completely naked, called in all the nurses to show them how a Jew is circumcised.
And he said he's going to kill me.
Verbally, he told me.
And he gave orders not to feed me.
And then finally, I don't know how it came about--
He punched me a few times, yes.
They put my hand in a cast, finally.
All the days of suffering.
And then put me into a barracks with a bunch of guys with their feet up in the air in casts.
And later on, I found out they were American paratroopers, American soldiers that their planes were shot down, and they had broken legs.
And they were treated pretty well, you know, by-- the nurses were nuns, German nuns.
And this doctor gave orders not to feed me.
And when he came in in the morning, of course, whoever are able to stand up, you had to stand up in attention.
And he would come over to me and ask me how I feel.
I said, my hand hurts.
He would punch me in the chest, that I fell back on the bed.
And then he would scream at me to stand at attention, to get up.
And if it wasn't for the American soldiers, these prisoners of war, I would have never survived it.
Because they convinced the nuns to get me out of there to send me back to my camp, which they did.
During the night, they would give me food.
They used to get care packages from the Red Cross.
And that's the first time I ate a Hershey bar.
And it was during the night.
If it wasn't for them, I would have probably starved there.
And then I was taken back you know, to my camp.
And I was doing logging, cutting trees, one hand with a hatchet, to chop it where which way the tree would fall.
And another guy, who only had one hand.
And with both of us--
He also had a broken--
A broken hand, so both--
Between the two us we made one person.
I told this guy to stop.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Is this picking up the sound?
[DRINKS]
Pardon me?
Yeah, Yiddish, I was able to talk to one of them.
Yeah.
And the Germans didn't know that.
No.
No, they didn't know.
I just told him my father was in Brooklyn, that he's in America.
And they told me not to worry.
You know, they'll-- they'll get me out of there, which they did.
That camp was a rough camp.
Because there, if somebody stole something, they lined us all up, and the SS with whips, beat that person until they collapsed and died, like execution style.
Who stole, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From-- after that, from there we heard the gunfires, you know, the war getting closer.
And then they told us to pack whatever we can carry.
And they was going to march us in deeper into Austria, away from the-- the border, because the Russians were there.
That's how we knew that the Russians were getting close.
People were running, you know, running away.
The Hungarians were coming through there.
The Austrians were running away.
And they marched us in deeper, you know, towards Austria until we got into a forest one night.
And we overpowered our guard.
And I believe-- you know, one of our guys shot the guard.
And we each run in different direction in the forest.
We were hiding out for quite a few days.
No.
No.
You know, you knew them because they were there.
They were from all over-- all over Hungary.
You don't know who they were.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's see, there was, yeah, one person that I knew.
I think his name was Goodman.
He was a heavyset man.
Because I used to do some of his work, and he gave me part of his bread ration.
Because I believe he was shot when my mother was shot.
He also, he died right on the spot.
Well, we-- we ran away.
We scattered in smaller-- small groups, without any guard or anything.
My mother and two sisters and maybe a couple other people.
We tried to stay in small groups.
We slept in the forest.
Of course, they were shooting at us from all directions.
No.
They were just shooting.
Because my sister was standing near a tree, and the tree got the bullet.
Because you know, the way the-- the bullet hit, then it chipped the tree.
It would have been very close.
She could have gotten it.
Yeah.
They were-- they knew there were people in the forest, you know, that people had escaped.
Yeah.
So they were firing just machine guns, anyway.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We traveled mostly at night, hoping that we're traveling in the right-- right direction.
We were traveling to the sound of the canons, of the tank firing and the noise.
During the night you can really hear it well.
We got into some abandoned farmhouses that we raided with food and clothing.
And finally, we came to an area where there were no soldiers.
It was just a road.
And we were walking, and somebody fired at us.
And that's how my mother caught a bullet.
On her left side, in the shoulder, which was near the heart.
And of course, she collapsed, but fully alert.
There was-- we saw a farmhouse from a distance.
I picked her up, and I carried her into the farmhouse and found a bed.
We put her into bed, and we waited.
All of a sudden, we saw German-- I mean Russian tanks, and the horse and buggies.
And I went out to ask for a doctor, a doctor.
And there was a doctor, a Russian officer, a Jew that spoke Yiddish.
So he came in, you know, to look at her.
And he gave me a handful of cotton, and put it on the wound, you know, to slow down the bleeding.
And he left.
And that's it.
He wouldn't do any more.
We stayed-- that that night we stayed in that house.
The Russians came.
I had to hide my sisters.
Because anything that moved, they raped.
They tried to attack my mother in bed.
I had to show her to them, that she's shot.
She's bleeding.
And finally--
Russian soldiers, animals.
Finally, daylight came.
I went outside.
I said to my sisters, we've got to get out of here.
We cannot stay here.
First of all, we were afraid the people may come back, you know, the people that owned the place.
I found a stray horse, and I caught him.
And I hitched him into-- into a wagon I found in a farm.
And I filled it up with hay and lay my mother in it.
And we traveled like that for [SOBBING] three days, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] three days and nights, through hell, through mud.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] She was hoping that by Friday she can light candles for the first time.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] In the midst of traveling, we met up with more Russians.
I had a better horse than they had.
They took it from me, and they gave me their sick horse.
I had to dress up my two sisters like old ladies, so they don't bother-- they don't bother them.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] We couldn't get no medication, or anybody to help us.
The closest thing we got to medication was a small village in Hungary, where we asked if there was a doctor in town.
The only thing there was was a midwife.
The midwife took us in.
She took my mother into the house, off the wagon.
And she was paralyzed already.
She couldn't pass water.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This was a Friday afternoon.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I went outside to unhitch the horse and try and find a stable to put him into until we see what happens, when are going to move on.
And when I came back to the house, she passed away.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
This was a Friday night, and it was time to-- time to light the candle.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Here three young kids, what do we do?
We had a dead mother.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So the midwife said-- she took us to the priest in the town.
Because there was no Jews ever lived in that town.
And to find out what-- you know, what we should do.
And we went and talked to the priest.
He was very nice.
There were no-- never any Jews lived there.
Most people didn't even know what a Jew was.
This was a Hungarian town called Felsomarac, a small village, a farming village.
Could you spell it?
No.
I was 14, and my younger sister was 13.
My older sister, 17.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The priest said that we can-- we can bury her.
I mean the war was still on, but not-- there there was-- the war was over.
That we could bury her in a corner of the Catholic cemetery, with the understanding that a year later we got to take her out on her first anniversary of her death.
And he took me to the town carpenter.
And I told him-- because you couldn't just buy a coffin.
You had to have it made-- that he should make me a strong coffin.
And I will pay him for it.
But I can't pay him now.
Because I have nothing.
But my father is in America.
And soon as things straighten out, and I'm able to be in touch with him, I will come and pay him for it.
And of course, I'm going to come back in one year to take her out, to take-- take her home.
And he made me a coffin.
And a couple of townspeople helped me dig a grave.
Because we had to dig a legal grave.
And my sisters and the midwife, they put her in a box.
And I went and got my horse to come in, you know, to take her to the cemetery.
Even the animal had the sense, didn't want to come into the yard.
That his ears went forward.
He knew there was something wrong.
That the person that he-- that he pulled, that he carried had died.
And we buried her.
I added in my little siddur-- I wish I had it now so that you can, you know, record it that I said [NON-ENGLISH].
[CRYING] [SIGHS] And I marked in the dates and the month of [NON-ENGLISH] when she died.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Then we continued on [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to-- on a horse and wagon towards a bigger area, where we can get a train to get to Budapest.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Finally, we got to a-- a bigger city.
I don't know what the name of it is.
And we sold the horse and the wagon.
And we got a few Hungarian forints.
And we jumped on a coal train, on a freight train that was going towards Budapest.
When we reached Budapest, at that railroad station, people were waiting for relatives to come-- come back.
I had nobody there.
Until my sister came down with the Fleck typhus-- typhus, with a high temperature.
I put her into a hospital.
And my kid sister and I, we were roaming the streets, looking for someplace to live, somebody that we knew.
Finally, somebody recognized me as Arnold Greenblatt.
He says, you're a Greenblatt boy.
And the man that recognized me was my stepmother's brother-in-law, who was now my stepmother.
Actually, his wife was a second cousin to my mother.
And he took me--
Pardon me?
His name was Stern, S-T-E-R-N.
Herman Stern.
Thank God he's still around.
He's here in America.
And I'm very close with him, and grateful for the rest of my life.
OK.
